coredata.dc:title: Effects of nitrogen/bismuth-doping on the photocatalyst composite of carbon dots/titanium dioxide nanoparticles (CDs/TNP) for enhanced visible light-driven removal of diclofenac
coredata.dc:creator: Hui, Khee Chung
Ang, Wei Lun
Yahya, Wan Zaireen Nisa
Sambudi, Nonni Soraya
coredata.dc:description: The present work demonstrates the coupling of titanium dioxide, TiO2 nanoparticles (TNP) with N-doped, Bi-doped, and N–Bi co-doped rice husk-derived carbon dots (CDs) via a facile dispersion method, forming respective photocatalyst composites of CDs/TNP, N-CDs/TNP, Bi-CDs/TNP and N–Bi-CDs/TNP. Characterization analyzes verified the successful incorporation of respective CDs samples into TNP, forming photocatalyst composite with narrowed band gap and quenched photoluminescence intensity. Photocatalytic activity of TNP and the respective composites was investigated for photodegradation of diclofenac (DCF) under both simulated sunlight and natural sunlight irradiation. The as-prepared N–Bi-CDs/TNP composite showed the best photocatalytic performance among all composites, able to completely degrade 5 ppm of DCF within 60 min and 180 min under both types of visible light irradiation, respectively. The N–Bi-CDs/TNP composite also showed a TOC removal efficiency up to 87.63%. N–Bi-CDs, worked as photosensitizer and electron reservoir, contributed to the outstanding photocatalytic activity of N–Bi-CDs/TNP, whereby the recombination was prolonged and light absorption was shifted towards the visible light region. Furthermore, the composite of N–Bi-CDs/TNP also demonstrated good stability and reusability over repeated degradation cycles. The photodegradation of DCF resulted into several intermediates, which were identified from LC-MS analysis. The present work could provide an insight on the application of heteroatoms doped and co-doped carbon dots in semiconductor oxide as high performance photocatalysts.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present work demonstrates the coupling of titanium dioxide, TiO2 nanoparticles (TNP) with N-doped, Bi-doped, and N–Bi co-doped rice husk-derived carbon dots (CDs) via a facile dispersion method, forming respective photocatalyst composites of CDs/TNP, N-CDs/TNP, Bi-CDs/TNP and N–Bi-CDs/TNP. Characterization analyzes verified the successful incorporation of respective CDs samples into TNP, forming photocatalyst composite with narrowed band gap and quenched photoluminescence intensity. Photocatalytic activity of TNP and the respective composites was investigated for photodegradation of diclofenac (DCF) under both simulated sunlight and natural sunlight irradiation. The as-prepared N–Bi-CDs/TNP composite showed the best photocatalytic performance among all composites, able to completely degrade 5 ppm of DCF within 60 min and 180 min under both types of visible light irradiation, respectively. The N–Bi-CDs/TNP composite also showed a TOC removal efficiency up to 87.63%. N–Bi-CDs, worked as photosensitizer and electron reservoir, contributed to the outstanding photocatalytic activity of N–Bi-CDs/TNP, whereby the recombination was prolonged and light absorption was shifted towards the visible light region. Furthermore, the composite of N–Bi-CDs/TNP also demonstrated good stability and reusability over repeated degradation cycles. The photodegradation of DCF resulted into several intermediates, which were identified from LC-MS analysis. The present work could provide an insight on the application of heteroatoms doped and co-doped carbon dots in semiconductor oxide as high performance photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Diclofenac (DCF), C14H11Cl2NO2, is an analgesic used for musculoskeletal injuries, osteoarthritis, joints’ pain, and inflammation (Kołodziejska and Kołodziejczyk, 2018). Based on a study to detect active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) in tap water of Putrajaya, Malaysia, DCF showed highest concentration up to 21.39 ngL−1 when compared to other pharmaceutical compounds (Wee et al., 2020). A study by Tuan Omar, et al. (2018) also reported that DCF gave the highest hazard index value when compared to other pharmaceutical compounds. Hence, the development of highly efficient, environmental-friendly treatment processes is at utmost importance to remove DCF. Conventional biological water treatment is ineffective in removing DCF due to poor biodegradable properties of DCF (González-González et al., 2022a; Verlicchi et al., 2012).

Heterogenous photocatalysis has emerged to be the one promising and effective method for the degradation of pharmaceutical compounds by utilizing light of certain wavelength. Titanium dioxide, TiO2 is one of the commonly used semiconductor metal oxide in photocatalytic applications due to its several advantages such as low cost, facile synthesis, and low toxicity (Moma and Baloyi, 2018). When TiO2 is exposed to the light source, energy that are equal or larger than the TiO2 band gap initiate the migration of electrons (e−) from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) of TiO2, and subsequent forming vacant holes (h+) in the conduction band (Martins et al., 2016; Wongso et al., 2020). Both electrons and holes undergo oxidation and reduction, respectively, forming highly reactive radicals. The reactive radicals will then react with the aromatic rings of the diclofenac (DCF), converting it into CO2, water, and other inorganic compounds (Bisaria et al., 2021; Mestre and Carvalho, 2019). However, rapid recombination of electrons-holes pair occurs in TiO2 due to the huge band gap energy of around 3.2 eV, causing the material to have low efficiency in visible light irradiation (Martins et al., 2016; Tian et al., 2015). Hence, modification of the structure of TiO2 or by coupling TiO2 with other semiconductors must be done to extend the light absorption of TiO2 from UV to visible region, thus improving the photocatalytic performance (Bisaria et al., 2021; Humayun et al., 2018).

Recently, the modification of TiO2 using doped carbon dots (CDs) has been discussed extensively. The unique optical and electronic properties of CDs such as strong light absorption, excellent electrons transfer ability, tunable photoluminescence and band gap, are important in the development of CDs-based photocatalytic system (González-González et al., 2022b). The bonding formed between the surface functional groups of doped CDs and TiO2 can lead to effective migration of electrons at the interfacial region of the composite, which inhibiting the recombination and promoting good photocatalytic activity of TiO2 (González-González et al., 2022b; Sun et al., 2019). Heteroatom doping helps in modifying the optical and electrical properties of carbon dots, allowing the CDs to function better under visible light irradiation (Xu et al., 2016). Heteroatom doping is a modification process in CDs leading to improvement in electronic properties through the addition of metal and non-metal elements into matrix of the CDs (Chung Hui et al., 2021; Kandasamy, 2019). Heteroatoms doping promotes larger band gap in the CDs for excitation, allowing the CDs to exhibit a green fluorescence emission (Jia et al., 2020; Wongso et al., 2020). Multiple heteroatoms co-doping has received much research attention recent years, due to the synergistic effect between heteroatom dopants which can give the CDs a unique electronic structure (Kandasamy, 2019; Xu et al., 2016).

In the case of non-metal-doped CDs, nitrogen-doped CDs have been proved as promising candidate to be paired with TiO2 due to similar size of nitrogen atom with carbon atom, and the presence of five valence electrons, which provides free electrons for the changes in photoluminescence and electronic properties of CDs (Liu et al., 2017; Shah et al., 2019). On the other hand, there is limited study regarding pairing of metal doped CDs with photocatalyst. To date, only Jamila et al. (2020) paired bismuth doped CDs with manganese oxide, Mn3O4 photocatalyst, with the photodegradation efficiency of Bromophenol blue is 37% higher than pristine Mn3O4. In the field of photocatalysis, bismuth has been a great alternative to noble metals and transition metals due to its lower cost and non-toxicity (Cappelletti et al., 2015; Odularu, 2020). Other than that, bismuth-based semiconductors such as bismuth vanadate and bismuth sulfide have been proved as good light-harvesting materials in photocatalytic applications (Ye et al., 2019). Thus, bismuth is proved as an excellent metal dopant in CDs as compared to other metals. The incorporation of heteroatom doped and co-doped CDs in TiO2 photocatalyst, not only narrows the band gap of TiO2, but also allows the TiO2 to have good photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation (Rahbar et al., 2019). Liu et al. (2020) found that the addition of N, P co-doped carbon quantum dots into TiO2, giving an outstanding methylene blue photodegradation performance which was 3.4 times higher than pristine TiO2. The N, P co-dopant played the roles as quantum wells to localize the photogenerated electrons from titanium dioxide, thus leading to reduction of recombination and outstanding photocatalytic activity. On the other hand, Koe, et al. (2020) reported the preparation of N, S co-doped CQDsTiO2 photocatalytic membrane for diclofenac removal, with a photodegradation performance of 58.97% higher than pure TiO2 membrane after 150 min of visible light irradiation. The outstanding photocatalytic performance was attributed to the function of N, S co-doping of CQDs which helped in lowering the working function of undoped CQDs, by creating larger barrier for the effective electrons transfer between CQDs and the titanium dioxide.

The pairing of doped and co-doped CDs with TiO2 is expected to narrow down the band gap energy of blank TiO2, prolong the recombination, and enhance the visible light photocatalytic activity of TiO2. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, this is the first report regarding the effect of non-metal doping, metal doping and non-metal/metal co-doping on the properties and photocatalytic activity of CDs/TiO2. Herein, this study investigates the application of nitrogen (N), bismuth (Bi) doping, and N–Bi co-doping in the composite of carbon dots/titanium dioxide nanoparticles (CDs/TNP), as well as their visible-light photodegradation performance in the removal of diclofenac (DCF). CDs were produced using dry rice husk pieces. Rice husk, as a commonly disposed biowaste from rice production process, contains high carbon content of 30–50% (Singh, 2018). Usage of rice husk as precursor for CDs intends to transform rice husk waste as a value-added feedstock for preparation of CDs. The synthesized CDs were then doped and co-doped using N and Bi precursor, respectively. The doped and co-doped CDs were paired with TiO2 nanoparticles (TNP), forming N-CDs/TNP, Bi-CDs/TNP and N–Bi-CDs/TNP composites, respectively. The performance of the as-fabricated composites was investigated in the photodegradation of DCF under both simulated sunlight and natural sunlight irradiation. Other than that, the reusability of the photocatalyst, photodegradation mechanism and proposed were also investigated.

Titanium isopropoxide (TTIP) (97%, Sigma Aldrich) was utilized as TiO2 precursor in the synthesis of TiO2 nanoparticles (TNP). Ethanol, C2H5OH (95%, HmbG), Nitric acid, HNO3 (70%, Sigma Aldrich) and sodium hydroxide, NaOH (Sigma Aldrich) were used as solvents for fabrication of TNP. Dry rice husks, taken from Jabatan Pertanian Perak, Malaysia, were utilized as biowaste precursors for the fabrication of CDs. Hydrochloric acid, HCl (37%, Fischer Scientific) was utilized as reagent in pre-treating the dry rice husks. Ethylenediamine (EDA, 99%, Sigma Aldrich) and bismuth (III) nitrate pentahydrate (Acros Organics) were utilized as chemical reagents for N-precursor and Bi-precursor, respectively. Methanol (Merck), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (Sigma Aldrich) and ascorbic acid (HmbG Chemicals) were used as reagents for radical trapping experiment. Deionized (DI) water was utilized throughout the experiment for the solution preparation, dilution and washing purpose. Diclofenac sodium (Acros Organics) was employed as pharmaceutical compound in the photodegradation study.

Titanium dioxide, TiO2 nanoparticles (TNP) were fabricated via a facile sol-gel hydrothermal method. 12 mL of TTIP, as the precursor of TNP preparation, was transferred into 50 mL of ethanol, and the mixture for stirred until a homogeneous white solution was formed. Then, 1 M of HNO3 was added dropwise to drop the pH to 2.0. The mixture was allowed to stir for another 1 h. Next, 20 mL of 12 M NaOH was mixed into the white solution, and the beaker containing the mixture was transferred into an ultrasonic water bath for sonication to take place for another 0.5 h. The mixture was then shifted into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for hydrothermal process at 100 °C for 36 h. The autoclave was then allowed to cool to room temperature. Following that, the pH of the precipitate was adjusted to approximately 7.0 by rinsing with 0.1 M of HCl, followed by DI water, repeatedly. The samples were then dried in laboratory oven at 80 °C for 12 h. Finally, the samples were calcined in a muffle furnace at a temperature of 400 °C for 2 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs/TNP, N-CDs/TNP, Bi-CDs/TNP and N–Bi-CDs/TNP composites
ce:section.ce:para: The composites were synthesized using a facile two-steps method. Rice husks were cleaned repeatedly using DI water to wash away the large impurities. Blending of the rice husks was done to transform the rice husks into smaller pieces. Following that, the blended rice husks were pre-treated with 0.10 M of HCl and rinsed with DI water repeatedly to wash away the remaining acids presented in them. After that, 500 mg of dry rice husk pieces, as carbon precursors were added with DI water as the solvent into the Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, for hydrothermal carbonization using hydrothermal operating conditions of 190 °C and 4 h. For synthesis of N-CDs, 5 mL of EDA, as N-precursor, was added to the mixture, before undergoing hydrothermal process at the same hydrothermal operating conditions. As for Bi-CDs, 25 mg of bismuth (III) nitrate pentahydrate, as Bi-precursor was dissolved into the mixture, before undergoing hydrothermal process using the same reacting conditions. Similarly, for synthesis of N–Bi-CDs, both N and Bi precursors are added in the mixture, before undergoing the hydrothermal process at the same reacting conditions. After being cooled to room temperature, all the CDs samples were filtered using a vacuum filter. The sample was then further purified by dialysis tubing with a cut-off molecular weight of 12 kDa for 24 h.

The composites of CDs/TNP were synthesized using a simple dispersion method. The as prepared TNP sample was mixed to a fixed amount of CDs solution (1 wt% with respect to TNP). DI water was added to top up the volume to 50 mL. The mixture was allowed to stir for 4 h, followed by an overnight drying in the laboratory oven at 60
°C
. The above steps were repeated for the synthesis of N-CDs/TNP, Bi-CDs/TNP and N–Bi-CDs/TNP composites.

Morphology of TNP and CDs/TNP composites was analyzed via Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) (Model: Tecnai G2 20 S-Twin). The lattice spacings of the samples were determined using the Image J software. The functional groups of the prepared catalysts were analyzed using Fourier-Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Model: PerkinElmer) with wavenumber of 500–4000 cm−1. The elemental compositions of the prepared catalysts were found via the X-ray photoelectron spectrophotometer (XPS) (Model: Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). Ultraviolet–visible diffusion reflectance spectra (UV–Vis DRS) analysis was done by using UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Model: Agilent Cary 8454) in the range of 200–800 nm. The crystallinity of samples was checked using X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD) on X'Pert3 Powder & Empyrean PANalytical with Cu Kα irradiation (λ = 1.54) with a range of diffraction angles (2θ) from 10 to 80° with step size of 1°/step and exposure time of 1 s/step. Finally, the photoluminescence (PL) properties of all the prepared photocatalysts composites were evaluated using an excitation wavelength of 350 nm via the photoluminescence spectrophotometer (Model: Edinburgh FLS920).

The performance of the TNP and respective CDs/TNP samples was investigated through the visible-driven photodegradation of diclofenac (DCF). For the simulated-sunlight degradation, the experiment was carried out using a 300 W Xenon arc lamp with a 420 nm cut-off filter as illuminant (Fluence rate = 70 mW/cm2). Following that, the natural sunlight degradation experiment using the as-fabricated TNP and respective CDs/TNP samples was carried out at atmospheric temperature on April 2021 at Bandar Seri Iskandar, Perak, Malaysia from 9.00 a.m. to 3.00 p.m. (Average UV Index of sunlight = 6). First and foremost, 50 mg of fabricated catalyst sample was transferred into 100 mL of prepared 5 ppm DCF solution. Then, it was stirred in dark condition for 0.5 h to achieve equilibrium. During the experiment, 6 mL of the liquid was collected from the mixture at fixed time intervals and centrifuged to filter the catalyst sample. The DCF concentration was checked from the measurement of absorbance of the supernatant (λ = 276 nm) using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Model: Agilent Cary 60). For the best performing composite, photodegradation of DCF was studied at three different initial concentrations (5, 10 and 25 ppm) under natural sunlight irradiation. To evaluate the mineralization rate of photodegradation, total organic carbon (TOC) before and after the experiment was measured using TOC Analyzer (Model: Analytic Jena NC/3100s). Other than that, reusability of the best performing composite was tested by performing the sunlight driven photodegradation for three consecutive cycles.

The analysis was performed using a Thermo Scientific C18 column (Acclaim™ Polar Advantage II, 3 × 150 mm, 3
μ
m particle size) on a Dionex UltiMate 3000 ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography (UHPLC) system. The mobile phase consisted of water with 0.1% formic acid (A) and 100% methanol (B). The gradient elution was performed at a flow rate of 0.4 mL/min with a total run time of 22 min. The injection volume was 3
μ
L. The gradient elution started at 5% B (0–3 min), 80% B (3–10 min), 80% B (10–15 min), and finally 5% B (15–22 min). High resolution mass spectrometry was carried out using a MicroTOF QIII Bruker Daltonic using an electrospray ionization (ESI) positive ionization with the following settings: capillary voltage 4500 V; nebulizer pressure 2.0 bar; drying gas flow rate 8 L/min; capillary temperature 300
°C
.

Fig. 1 shows the HRTEM images of TNP and the respective CDs/TNP composite. All the samples exhibit irregular crystal shape. At low magnification, presence of CDs is hard to be determined due to their small size and amorphous nature. Further observation is done at a higher magnification as shown in Fig. 1(f–j). Two different ranges of lattice fringe spacing are found, and measured to be 0.13–0.16 nm and 0.28–0.42 nm, respectively. Lattice spacing of synthesized pristine TiO2 nanoparticles (TNP) is found to be 0.15 nm. The lattice spacings at the range from 0.28 to 0.42 nm are attributed to the (002) diffraction planes of graphitic carbon (JCDPS card no: 75–1621) (Prasath et al., 2019; Qu et al., 2014). The difference in the two ranges of lattice spacing determined at the TNP interface in the HRTEM images reveals that the CDs, N-CDs, Bi-CDs, and N–Bi-CDs are successfully incorporated into the matrix of TNP, respectively. Recently, Deng, et al. (2021) had synthesized CDs/TiO2 and verified the presence of CDs in HRTEM using the similar way.

Fig. 2 shows the FTIR spectra of blank TNP as well as the doped and co-doped CDs/TNP composites. The existence of typical functional groups of titanium dioxide and the composites is revealed. FTIR detects a broad peak at 3369 cm−1, corresponded to
−
OH stretching from the adsorbed water (Deng et al., 2021; Wongso et al., 2020). Weak bending vibration of –OH groups is determined at 1626 cm−1 (Deng et al., 2021). The Ti–O–Ti, Ti–O–C and Ti–O-peaks are found at the range of 1000 to 400 cm−1 (Cheshme Khavar et al., 2018; Ren et al., 2017; Wongso et al., 2020). N–H bending is found at peaks of 1490 cm−1 for N-CDs/TNP and N–Bi-CDs/TNP, proving the presence of functional groups related to N-CDs in the composites (Tadesse et al., 2018). However, FTIR cannot provide information on the presence of bismuth atoms in the as-prepared samples, which will be further analyzed using XPS technique.

Fig. 3 shows the XPS survey spectra of the synthesized photocatalyst samples. Element compositions of all the samples are presented in Table 1 . From the XPS survey spectra, main peaks of O 1s, Ti2p, N1s, C1s and Bi4f are determined at binding energies of 525, 460, 400, 286 and 160 eV, respectively. XPS high resolution spectra of all the samples are presented in Fig. S1-S5. Ti2p spectra (Fig. S1) have two major peaks at 458–460 eV and 464–466 eV, respectively, attributed to Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2 peaks of Ti4+ ions (Bharti et al., 2016). A small shoulder is determined at binding energy of 460–462 eV, which ascribed to Ti2p1/2 peak of Ti3+ ions. Formation of Ti3+ ions is a surface defect caused by lattice distortion in the TNP and other respective CDs/TNP composites (Xu et al., 2017). Lattice distortion is caused by presence of chloride ions which lead to surface stresses that disrupt the crystal structure of TNP (Xu et al., 2017). As the washing process of the TNP samples involves hydrochloric acid as neutralization agent, there might be a possibility that there is presence of unremoved acid in the TNP samples at a very little amount. Fig. S2 depicts the O1s spectra for TNP and respective CDs/TNP composites. The O 1s spectra are deconvoluted into two main peaks located at 530–532 eV and 532–533 eV, which corresponded to oxygen vacancies and Ti–OH bonding, respectively (Fan et al., 2015; Li et al., 2014). Oxygen vacancies are attributed to the lattice distortion of TiO2 that leads to greater bond length and lower bonding energy in the TiO2 (Xu et al., 2017; Ye et al., 2011). C1s spectra in Fig. S3 detect peaks at 286–287 eV, 287–288 eV, and 289–290 eV, corresponded to C–O bonding, CO bonding and OC–O bonding, respectively (Jin et al., 2019). N1s high resolution spectra of N-CDs/TNP and N–Bi-CDs/TNP are presented in Fig. S4, showing peaks at 400.14, 401 and 402.50 eV, corresponded to N–Ti–O, CN and graphitic N, which proved the existence of N-dopant in the respective CDs/TNP composites (Jia et al., 2017; Martins et al., 2016). Next, Bi4f high resolution spectra of Bi-CDs/TNP and N–Bi-CDs/TNP are depicted in Fig. S5. Small peaks are observed at 160–161 eV and 165–166 eV, corresponded to the Bi4f7/2 and Bi4f5/2 of Bi3+ ions, indicating that the bismuth is existing in the form of bismuth (III) oxide (Jamila et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2017). This reveals the successful doping of bismuth element into the CDs. The recent study by Jamila et al. (2020) also confirmed the successful doping of CDs and formation of CDs/Mn3O4 through peak of Bi3+ in the XPS analysis. Hence, from the determined bonding in XPS spectra, it is proven the successful doping of CDs, as well as the successful formation of respective CDs/TNP composites.

Fig. 4 depicts the XRD spectra of the synthesized TNP and respective CDs/TNP composites. Peaks at 25.5°, 38.0°, 48.3°, 54.0°, 55.1° and 62.5° are corresponded to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211) and (204) crystal planes of anatase TiO2 with JCDPS card no of 21–1272 (Deng et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2018). The addition of CDs into TNP does not change the pattern of XRD spectra. The studies by Martins et al. (2016) and Wongso et al. (2020) exhibit similar behavior in which the XRD peaks of carbon are not found due to its amorphous nature and small quantity added in the composites.

Fig. 5 shows the UV–Vis DRS spectra and the band gap energy estimation of all the synthesized TNP and respective CDs/TNP samples. For the pure TNP sample, using the Tauc plot, the band gap energy is determined to be 3.00 eV. For the composites, the band gap energies are found to be 2.60, 2.50, 1.30 and 2.40 eV for CDs/TNP, N-CDs/TNP, Bi-CDs/TNP and N–Bi-CDs/TNP composites, respectively. Presence of the CDs in TNP sample serves as transporting channel for the photogenerated electrons to dwell inside, which promotes the charge separation in TNP, thus limiting the recombination in TNP (Hua et al., 2020). The absorption in the visible light region increases upon incorporation of N-CDs into TNP. Introduction of N-dopant into CDs causes charge delocalization and helps in lower the working function of undoped CDs (Martins et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2013). As a result, band gap of N-CDs/TNP will be much lower as compared to pristine TNP and CDs/TNP, due to more effective electrons transfer between CDs and TNP interface. On the other hand, N–Bi-CDs/TNP composite displays a much lower band gap than that of the CDs/TNP and N-CDs/TNP composites. Similarly, N–Bi-CDs aim to lower the working function of single element doped CDs and undoped CDs by creating more quantum wells for electrons to dwell in, thus leading to delay of recombination and lower band gap energy of TNP (Liu et al., 2020). In other words, heteroatom co-doping plays a role in narrowing the band gap of titanium dioxide, which attributed by the charge redistribution in the carbon dots (Jin et al., 2013; Sakdaronnarong et al., 2020). For Bi-CDs/TNP, the estimated band gap showed the lowest value, which could be explained by the agglomeration of Bi-CDs in the Bi-CDs/TNP composite, causing the light to scatter (Jamila et al., 2020). Overall, it is proven that the heteroatoms doping of carbon quantum dots, not only plays its role to reduce the band gap energy of pristine TNP, but also enhanced the visible light absorption of TNP.

Fig. 6 shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the synthesized TNP and respective CDs/TNP composites. Compared with the pure TNP, CDs/TNP, N-CDs/TNP, Bi-CDs/TNP and N–Bi-CDs/TNP composites display decreased PL emission. The quenched PL emission reveals the inhibition of charge recombination in pristine TNP sample upon addition of the CDs, N-CDs, Bi-CDs and N–Bi-CDs, respectively (Lettieri et al., 2021). In this case, the CDs in the CDs/TNP composites, are performing their role as photosensitizers and electron trappers, which lengthen the lifetime for the charge separation in pristine TNP sample (Wongso et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2013). Upon incorporation N-CDs, Bi-CDs, and N–Bi-CDs in TNP, the PL emission is quenched to lower values, which can be attributed to the function of the dopants as quantum wells to trap the photogenerated electrons (Liu et al., 2020; Martins et al., 2016). Hence, effective charge separation can occur in TNP due to delayed charge recombination.

The synthesized TNP, CDs/TNP, N-CDs/TNP, Bi-CDs/TNP and N–Bi-CDs/TNP samples were tested for the photodegradation of DCF under both simulated sunlight and natural sunlight irradiation. Fig. 7 (a) and Fig. 7 (b) show the photocatalytic performance of the five samples in DCF under a 300 W Xenon lamp and under sunlight irradiation, respectively. In the absence of photocatalyst, DCF is barely being removed under sunlight irradiation. The result suggests that DCF is stable in water under both simulated sunlight and sunlight irradiation. Under the 300 W Xenon lamp irradiation, the photodegradation rate follows the order of N–Bi-CDs/TNP > N-CDs/TNP > CDs/TNP > Bi-CDs/TNP > TNP. Pristine TNP sample shows 89.5% DCF removal efficiency. CDs/TNP gives a faster degradation rate, able to remove DCF completely after 120 min of light irradiation under 300 W Xenon lamp. Both N-CDs/TNP and N–Bi-CDs/TNP composites can remove DCF completely within 60 min, with the latter shows a slightly faster degradation rate.

On the other hand, for the natural sunlight degradation of DCF, the photodegradation rate also follows the order of N–Bi-CDs/TNP > N-CDs/TNP > CDs/TNP > Bi-CDs/TNP > TNP. N–Bi-CDs/TNP shows the best performance, able to remove DCF completely within 180 min of sunlight irradiation. Photodegradation of DCF using N–Bi-CDs/TNP is 27.5% higher than pristine TNP, after 360 min of sunlight irradiation. N–Bi-CDs/TNP also shows fastest degradation rate, which is 60 min faster than CDs/TNP and N-CDs/TNP. Large band gap of TNP causes recombination of electron-hole pairs. CDs play the role of photosensitizers and electron reservoirs, whereby the photogenerated electrons from conduction band in TNP will move towards the CDs, thus delaying the recombination in TNP (Hu et al., 2018; Wongso et al., 2020). As revealed in the PL spectra, addition of CDs prolongs the recombination in pristine TNP. Using N-doping and N–Bi co-doping, the light absorption of TNP is extended towards visible light region, enabling the composites to perform better under visible light irradiation. The dopants also play the role as quantum wells to localize the photogenerated electrons from TNP, thus delaying the recombination in TNP and leading to enhanced photocatalytic activity (Liu et al., 2020). The double elements co-doping provides a synergistic effect in enhancing the photodegradation of DCF. Recent studies by Ke et al. (2014) and Koe et al. (2020) revealed that co-doped CDs lowered the work function of undoped CDs and single-element doped CDs, by forming large barrier between the co-doped CDs and titanium dioxide, and leading to effective electrons transfer between the CDs and the interface of titanium dioxide. As a result, more reactive radical species were generated, resulting in higher photodegradation efficiency (Ke et al., 2014; Koe et al., 2020). Other than that, the study by Teymoorian et al. (2021) revealed that co-doping had the capability of reducing agglomeration in the synthesized photocatalyst, thus leading to enhanced photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst. In the case of DCF degradation using Bi-CDs/TNP, only 82.5% and 78.5% DCF removal efficiencies are achieved after 120 min of 300 W Xenon lamp irradiation, and after 360 min of sunlight irradiation, respectively. This can be attributed to the very low band gap of Bi-CDs/TNP composite as revealed in UV–Vis spectra. The preferable band gap of photocatalyst should be in the range of 2.0–3.0 eV, so that the charge separation and the radical formation can occur (Dincer and Zamfirescu, 2016). Since the band gap of Bi-CDs/TNP is 1.30 eV as revealed in UV–Vis spectra, it is possible that the band gap energy is not enough for the catalyst to be activated by the visible light source, thus leading to low photocatalytic activity of Bi-CDs/TNP. Overall, it can be observed that the complete removal of DCF happens faster under simulated sunlight in the laboratory as compared to natural sunlight, which is due to constant light intensity throughout the simulated sunlight experiment. Interestingly, under simulated sunlight photodegradation of DCF, N-CDs/TNP and N–Bi-CDs/TNP gives an almost similar photocatalytic removal performance, with the latter shows a slightly faster degradation rate. On the other hand, N–Bi-CDs/TNP presents a clearly faster rate than N-CDs/TNP. The contrast in the performance of the two samples under the two different light sources can be explained by surrounding oxygen of the natural sunlight photodegradation. As the natural sunlight photodegradation is carried out under atmospheric conditions, the surrounding oxygen might have contributed to the faster rate of photodegradation of DCF under natural sunlight irradiation. There are more reactions between photogenerated electrons and the surface oxygen, leading to generation of more superoxide radicals (Su et al., 2017). As a result, DCF photodegradation under natural sunlight among the respective samples shows a clearer difference in term of rate as there is presence of more reactive species in removing the DCF. A recent work by Su et al. (2017) also exhibited similar performance whereby the photodegradation of methylene blue showed faster performance under oxygen atmosphere.

Finally, the stability of N–Bi-CDs/TNP is evaluated from the reusability of the photocatalyst. At the end of each cycle of the degradation experiment, the catalyst sample was reused without undergoing any treatment. Fig. 8c shows that the composite of N–Bi-CDs/TNP maintains the performance of 100% removal efficiency of 5 ppm degradation after three consecutive cycles of experiment under natural sunlight irradiation. The slightly slower degradation in cycles 2 and 3 is accumulation of intermediate products at the catalyst surface after long duration of photodegradation, which leads to reduction in active sites in the photocatalyst (Mugunthan et al., 2017). Even though in cycles 2 and 3, the removal efficiencies are slightly slower as compared to cycle 1, the composite is still considered having a good stability, as DCF was completely removed before the ending of each cycle. Moreover, XRD spectra and FTIR spectra of N–Bi-CDs/TNP do not show any major change in functionality and crystallinity (Fig. 9 ), indicating remarkable stability of the synthesized photocatalyst after 3 consecutive cycles of photodegradation experiment under natural sunlight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed photodegradation mechanism of DCF and reactive species detection
ce:section.ce:para: A proposed mechanism of DCF degradation using N–Bi-CDs/TNP is shown in Fig. 10 . When the surface of N–Bi-CDs/TNP photocatalyst is strike by the sunlight, separation of electrons and holes will occur when the photon energy is larger or equal to the band gap of the photocatalyst. Electrons will migrate from the valence band (VB) of the TNP, to the conduction band (CB) of TNP, thus leaving positive charged holes at the VB of TNP. Then, electrons migrate to the VB of the N–Bi-CDs, which further excite to the CB of N–Bi-CDs. The CDs, in this case, work as electron reservoirs which help in reducing the recombination rate of TNP. The functions of N-doping and Bi-doping are to create quantum wells which served as sites for the photogenerated electrons to dwell in (Liu et al., 2020). In the case of N–Bi-CDs, the N–Bi co-doping aim to lower the working function of undoped and single-element doped CDs by creating more quantum wells to facilitate the transfer of electrons between the CDs and TNP interface. As a result, effective charge separation can occur in the TNP, thus improving the formation of reactive species in the TNP. Holes at the VB of TNP will react with the surface water, forming hydroxyl radicals (•OH). At the same time, electrons at the CB of TNP react with the air, forming superoxide radicals (•O2 −). These reactive species will be responsible in the degradation of the DCF molecules.

Radical trapping experiment was conducted to confirm the presence of reactive species in the photodegradation of DCF. Fig. 11 shows the effects of different radical scavengers on the photodegradation of DCF. The main reactive species in the photodegradation of DCF are holes (h+), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and superoxide radicals (•O2 −). Ascorbic acid, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and methanol was used as superoxide scavenger, hole scavenger and hydroxyl scavenger, respectively (Chaudhari et al., 2021; Elangovan et al., 2021; León et al., 2021). 1 mM of the scavenger was added in the reaction system in the presence of N–Bi-CDs/TNP composite. After 180 min of sunlight irradiation, the degradation rate of DCF using N–Bi-CDs/TNP composite is suppressed remarkably upon addition of the respective scavengers. The degradation rate follows the order: No scavenger > Ascorbic acid > EDTA > Methanol. This proves the involvement of holes, superoxide radicals and hydroxyl radicals as dominant reactive species in the photodegradation of DCF.

To determine the intermediates formed during the DCF photodegradation process using N–Bi-CDs/TNP under natural sunlight irradiation, DCF photodegradation sample was taken at different time intervals and analyzed for the products under LCMS analysis. The detected intermediates are tabulated in Table 3 . LCMS/MS chromatograms of the detected intermediates are presented in Fig. S6-S9. Based on the time intervals of the sample collection, intermediate 1, 2 and 4 were detected within the first 60 min of the sunlight photodegradation. Intermediate 3 was found after 180 min of DCF photodegradation under natural sunlight. The structure of the found intermediates was assessed based on the accurate mass to charge-ratio (m/z). Generally, three degradation pathways were observed, which involved decarboxylation and dehalogenation as the major reactions. The possible degradation pathway is depicted in Fig. 12 . In Path 1, decarboxylation of aliphatic chain of DCF (m/z 295) takes place, leading to loss of the –COOH group and forming Intermediate 1 (m/z 251). This follows by dehalogenation of the carbazole molecule which involves the loss of chlorine and hydrogen atoms, as well as rearrangement of structure to form a new ring as observed in Intermediate 3 (m/z 215). Alternatively, as shown in Path 3, dehalogenation of DCF may occur first, forming Intermediate 2 (m/z 259). Subsequently, Intermediate 2 undergoes decarboxylation reaction to form Intermediate 3 (m/z 215). Finally, Path 2 is proposed which involves the two consequent dehalogenation reactions in DCF in removing the two chlorine atoms, forming Intermediate 2 (m/z 259) and Intermediate 4 (m/z 225), respectively. These formed intermediates and proposed pathways agree with the previous studies (Gou et al., 2017; Martínez et al., 2011; Mugunthan et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2013).

In conclusion, visible-driven photocatalysts of CDs/TNP, N-CDs/TNP, Bi-CDs/TNP and N–Bi-CDs/TNP were successfully prepared in this study for the removal of diclofenac (DCF). The photodegradation performance is related to the band gap and charge recombination of the sample. CDs play the role as photosensitizers and electron reservoirs, which delay the recombination and enhance the photocatalytic activity of pristine TNP sample. As proven by UV–Vis DRS and PL spectra, incorporation of N-CDs, Bi-CDs and N–Bi-CDs broaden the visible light absorption of TNP, leading to quenched PL emission and narrowed band gap energies, thus prolonged the charge recombination of pure TNP. Presence of N–Bi co-doping in CDs lowers the working function of undoped CDs and single element-doped CDs by providing more quantum wells to localize the photogenerated electrons. As a result, it allows effective transfer of electrons between CDs/TNP interface. Subsequently, effective charge separation occurs in the TNP, leading to higher photocatalytic activity. It was found that N–Bi-CDs/TNP showed highest photocatalytic activity, able to completely remove DCF after a period of 60 min and 180 min under simulated sunlight and natural sunlight irradiation, respectively. The N–Bi-CDs/TNP composite also showed good stability and reusability, whereby the photodegradation performance maintained 100% DCF removal for three consecutive cycles. Finally, photodegradation mechanism along with the photodegradation intermediates was also established, which mainly involved decarboxylation and dehalogenation as the major reactions. In summary, N–Bi-CDs/TNP shows great potential in the treatment of pharmaceutical wastewater, which can be explored thoroughly in the future.
